overview.name: Notebook
overview.description: Notebooks are essential stationery products widely used in India by students, professionals, and institutions for writing, drawing, and record-keeping. The Indian notebook market is characterized by a mix of organized players offering branded products and a large unorganized sector providing affordable options. The industry relies heavily on paper manufacturing, which has significant environmental considerations in India.
overview.origin_in_india: The heartland of paper and notebook manufacturing in India spans states like Maharashtra, Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Odisha, driven by raw material availability and industrial infrastructure.
key_organisations: Tamil Nadu Newsprint and Papers Limited (TNPL)
overview.historical_note: The first paper factory in India was established in 1812 in Serampore, West Bengal. Mechanized paper manufacturing began in the 1830s near Calcutta. The Titagarh Paper Mills, built in 1882, became the first large-scale paper manufacturing plant in India. ITC launched its Classmate brand in 2003, while Navneet Education, founded in 1959, expanded into stationery manufacturing with a facility in Daman in 1994-1995.

raw_materials.name: Paper (Writing & Printing Grade)
raw_materials.source_region: Maharashtra, Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Odisha, Punjab, Haryana, Assam.
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 70-82 per kg (for 60-90 GSM, as of July 2025), Rs 49-77 per kg (for Maplitho paper, as of April 2026)
raw_materials.notes: Sourced from domestic paper mills. Raw material for paper includes wood pulp, bamboo, bagasse, wheat straw, and recycled paper.

raw_materials.name: Pulpwood
raw_materials.source_region: Forest-rich regions of India, often through farm forestry programs.
raw_materials.notes: Wood pulp prices surged to USD 650 per metric ton (approx. Rs 54,000 per MT) in April 2026 due to supply chain disruptions and geopolitical issues.

raw_materials.name: Bagasse/Agricultural Waste
raw_materials.source_region: Sugarcane producing states like Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu.
raw_materials.notes: Cost-effective raw material, but subject to seasonal availability. TNPL uses bagasse for paper production.

raw_materials.name: Cover Material (Cardstock/Paperboard)
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 3.50 per board (for 220 GSM laminated with printing, as of Oct 2025, for a 295x210mm notebook)
raw_materials.notes: Often laminated and pre-printed with designs. Recycled materials are also used.

raw_materials.name: Stitching Wire/Binding Wire
raw_materials.source_region: Manufacturers in Maharashtra (Vasai), Gujarat (Vapi), West Bengal (Kolkata), Delhi.
raw_materials.cost_per_unit: Rs 80-120 per kg (Galvanised Iron, SWG 18-24, as of June 2026)
raw_materials.notes: Used for saddle stitching or center pinning. Available in various gauges (e.g., 18-30 SWG).

raw_materials.name: Glue/Adhesive
raw_materials.notes: Used for perfect binding or securing covers.

supply_chain.location: Forests (for wood/bamboo), agricultural fields (for bagasse/wheat straw), urban centers (for waste paper) across India.
supply_chain.description: Wood and bamboo are harvested from forests, often through farm forestry programs. Agricultural residues like bagasse and wheat straw are collected from farms. Waste paper is collected from various sources by waste pickers and recycling centers. These raw materials are then transported to paper mills.

supply_chain.location: Major paper mills in Maharashtra (e.g., Pune, Ballarpur), Gujarat (e.g., Vapi, Morbi), Uttar Pradesh (e.g., Saharanpur, Lalkuan), Andhra Pradesh (e.g., Sarapaka, Rajahmundry), Tamil Nadu (e.g., Kagithapuram), Karnataka (e.g., Dandeli), West Bengal (e.g., Titagarh), Odisha (e.g., Rayagada).
supply_chain.description: Raw materials are processed into pulp through mechanical or chemical methods. The pulp is then bleached (often using Elemental Chlorine Free - ECF technology) and fed into large paper machines. These machines dewater the pulp, press it, and dry it to form large rolls of paper. Water consumption is high, but the industry has significantly reduced it.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 40-50 lakh per TPD (for capital expenditure). Operational costs for paper manufacturing can be significant, with raw material (pulp) being a major component.

supply_chain.location: Paper mills or specialized printing and stationery manufacturing units across industrial clusters in states like Maharashtra, Gujarat, Delhi-NCR, Tamil Nadu, West Bengal.
supply_chain.description: Large paper rolls are cut into sheets of specific sizes. These sheets are then ruled (single line, double line, square, unruled) and sometimes printed with logos or designs for covers using precision printing presses. Covers are also printed and often laminated.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 1-5 per notebook (for printing and cutting, depending on complexity and volume)

supply_chain.location: Small, medium, and large-scale notebook manufacturing units across India, including MSMEs in industrial areas and smaller towns.
supply_chain.description: The cut and ruled paper sheets are collated, covers are added, and then bound using various methods like saddle stitching (pinning), perfect binding (gluing), or spiral binding. Edges are squared for a neat finish. Quality control checks are performed at various stages.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 2-10 per notebook (for assembly, binding, and finishing, depending on binding type and automation level)

supply_chain.location: Manufacturing units, regional warehouses, wholesale markets, retail stores across India.
supply_chain.description: Finished notebooks are packed into bundles, cartons, and then transported to distributors, wholesalers, and retailers nationwide. This involves a vast network reaching urban and rural areas.
supply_chain.cost_added_inr: Rs 1-5 per notebook (for packaging, logistics, and distribution margins)

major_clusters: Vapi (Gujarat) - Shree Ajit Pulp & Paper Limited
Mumbai (Maharashtra) - Navneet Education headquarters and distribution
trade_routes: National Highways and railway networks connecting paper mills to major consumption centers (e.g., Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Bengaluru).
Coastal shipping for inter-state movement of raw materials and finished goods.
Local distribution networks (road transport) from wholesale hubs to retail outlets.

economics.retail_price_inr: Rs 10-260 per notebook (depending on brand, size, pages, and binding)

cost_breakdown.cost_inr: Rs 20-35 per notebook (for a 120-172 page notebook, based on paper cost of Rs 70-80/kg and approx 100kg paper for 300 notebooks)
Rs 4-8 per notebook (cover Rs 3.50, wire Rs 0.90 per 300 notebooks, glue minimal)
Rs 1-3 per notebook (based on daily labor cost of Rs 2,923 for 2400 notebooks)
Rs 0.15-0.20 per notebook (based on Rs 400/day for 2400 notebooks)

economics.gst_rate: 0% (on notebooks, exercise books, and registers after September 22, 2025)
economics.gst_notes: Prior to September 22, 2025, notebooks attracted 12% GST. However, raw materials like paper and printing services are taxed at 18% GST. This creates an inverted duty structure, making manufacturers ineligible for claiming Input Tax Credit (ITC) on raw materials, which can lead to embedded taxes in the production cost and potentially higher retail prices.
economics.market_size_india: USD 2.61 billion (2024), projected to reach USD 4.02 billion by 2030 with a CAGR of 5.64%. Other estimates suggest USD 6 billion by 2025 with 5-15% CAGR. Paper stationery accounts for about 42% of the market (approx. Rs 162 billion).

retail_prices.product_name: Case Bound Note Book for Executives
retail_prices.pack_size: 240 Pages, A4 Size (14.8 x 21 cm), Single Line

sustainability.carbon_footprint: High, primarily due to energy-intensive pulping and paper manufacturing processes. Efforts are being made to integrate renewable energy sources.
sustainability.water_usage: High in paper manufacturing, but the Indian paper industry has achieved an impressive 80% reduction in water consumption in recent years, with ongoing efforts towards zero-liquid discharge (ZLD) mechanisms.
key_concerns: Raw material scarcity (wood and bamboo) leading to reliance on imports or agro-residues.
Water pollution from effluent discharge, though stringent norms and treatment technologies are being adopted.
High energy consumption in paper mills, driving efforts towards energy efficiency and renewable sources.
Waste generation from paper trimmings and packaging materials.
certifications_schemes: FSC (Forest Stewardship Council) certification for sustainably sourced paper.
ECF (Elemental Chlorine Free) technology for bleaching pulp to reduce toxicity.
Farm forestry programs by paper manufacturers to plant trees and ensure raw material availability.
Increased use of recycled fiber and agricultural waste (bagasse, wheat straw) to reduce reliance on virgin wood pulp.

key_schemes: Prime Minister's Employment Generation Programme (PMEGP): Offers subsidies (up to 35% for rural, 25% for urban) and project funding up to Rs 25 lakh for new manufacturing entrepreneurs, including paper cup units (relevant for small-scale stationery).
Stand-Up India Scheme: Provides loans between Rs 10 lakh to Rs 1 crore for women and SC/ST entrepreneurs for greenfield projects.
MUDRA Loans under PMMY (Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana): Offers loans up to Rs 10 lakh (Shishu, Kishore, Tarun categories) for micro-enterprises.
Technology Upgradation Fund (TUF) Scheme: Benefits are being extended to the paper industry to encourage modernization and combat technology obsolescence.
National Packaging Initiative: Focuses on promoting sustainable packaging solutions and improving efficiency, indirectly benefiting paperboard and cover material production.
MSME Udyam Registration: Facilitates access to various government support and subsidies for Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises.
government_policy.regulatory_body: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) - Sets environmental norms for paper industries.
government_policy.import_export_policy: The Indian government imposed a Minimum Import Price (MIP) of Rs 67,220 per ton on virgin multi-layer paperboard (VPB) effective until March 31, 2026, to protect domestic mills. There are concerns about large importers flooding Indian markets with finished notebooks from ASEAN countries. The industry has recommended 0% GST for wood procured by paper manufacturers and 18% GST on the import of paper to discourage dumping.
